section.title: The Snare of the Adulteress
section.sectionRationale: The theme begun in chapter 5 reaches its fullest and most dramatic treatment here. Parental instruction against the adulteress, 6:20 to 24, moves into the devastating cost of adultery, 6:25 to 35, then a fresh appeal to treasure wisdom as a close kinswoman, 7:1 to 5, before the discourse becomes a narrated scene, a father watching from his window as a naive young man is lured step by step to his own destruction, 7:6 to 27.
section.thesis: The adulteress promises pleasure but delivers fire, wounds, and a road to death, and the only sure guard against her is treasuring wisdom's instruction as intimately as one treasures family, since a heart without that guard will, like the watched young man, walk unknowingly into the slaughter.
section.frameNote: Five movements: parental instruction against the adulteress, 6:20 to 24; the devastating cost of adultery, 6:25 to 35; the appeal to keep wisdom as close as family, 7:1 to 5; the narrated scene of a young man's seduction, 7:6 to 23, the section's dramatic climax; and the closing warning that her house is the road to Sheol, 7:24 to 27.

verses.text: My son, keep your father's commandment, and do not forsake your mother's teaching.
Bind them always upon your heart; tie them around your neck.
When you walk, they will guide you; when you lie down, they will watch over you; when you awake, they will speak to you.
For this commandment is a lamp, this teaching is a light, and the reproofs of discipline are the way to life,
to keep you from the evil woman, from the smooth tongue of the {{nokriyah|adulteress}}.
Do not lust in your heart for her beauty or let her captivate you with her eyes.
For the levy of the prostitute is poverty, and the adulteress preys upon your very life.
Can a man embrace fire and his clothes not be burned?
Can a man walk on hot coals without scorching his feet?
So is he who sleeps with another man's wife; no one who touches her will go unpunished.
Men do not despise the thief if he steals to satisfy his hunger.
Yet if caught, he must pay sevenfold; he must give up all the wealth of his house.
He who commits adultery lacks judgment; whoever does so destroys himself.
Wounds and dishonor will befall him, and his reproach will never be wiped away.
For {{qinah|jealousy}} enrages a husband, and he will show no mercy in the day of vengeance.
He will not be appeased by any ransom, or persuaded by lavish gifts.

units.label: Keep wisdom as close as family

verses.text: My son, keep my words and treasure my commandments within you.
Keep my commandments and live; guard my teachings as the apple of your eye.
Tie them to your fingers; write them on the tablet of your heart.
Say to wisdom, "You are my sister," and call understanding your kinsman,
that they may keep you from the adulteress, from the stranger with seductive words.

units.label: A young man watched and lured

verses.text: For at the window of my house I looked through the lattice.
I saw among the simple, I noticed among the youths, a {{peti|young man lacking judgment}},
crossing the street near her corner, strolling down the road to her house,
at twilight, as the day was fading into the dark of the night.
Then a woman came out to meet him, with the attire of a harlot and cunning of heart.
She is loud and defiant; her feet do not remain at home.
Now in the street, now in the squares, she lurks at every corner.
She seizes him and kisses him; she brazenly says to him:
"I have made my peace offerings; today I have paid my vows.
So I came out to meet you; I sought you, and I have found you.
I have decked my bed with coverings, with colored linen from Egypt.
I have perfumed my bed with myrrh, with aloes, and with cinnamon.
Come, let us take our fill of love till morning. Let us delight in loving caresses!
For my husband is not at home; he has gone on a long journey.
He took with him a bag of money and will not return till the moon is full."
With her great persuasion she entices him; with her flattering lips she lures him.
He follows her on impulse, like an ox going to the slaughter, like a deer bounding into a trap,
until an arrow pierces his liver, like a bird darting into a snare, not knowing it will cost him his life.

units.label: Her house is the road to Sheol

verses.text: Now, my sons, listen to me, and attend to the words of my mouth.
Do not let your heart turn aside to her ways; do not stray into her paths.
For she has brought many down to death; her slain are many in number.
Her house is the road to Sheol, descending to the chambers of death.

nokriyah.gloss: The foreign or adulterous woman, literally the one made a stranger through covenant unfaithfulness. Her smooth tongue, 6:24, and seductive words, 7:5, are named as the very dangers a father's teaching is meant to guard against.

qinah.gloss: Jealousy, here the righteous, unappeasable anger of a betrayed husband, 6:34. The word can describe both destructive envy and righteous zeal for what is rightfully one's own, and here it names the very real, human cost adultery inflicts on a marriage.

peti.gloss: The naive or simple one, here a young man utterly lacking judgment, 7:7, watched from a window as he walks unknowingly toward his own ruin. His folly is not stupidity but a failure to see danger clearly, exactly what wisdom's instruction exists to correct.

basic.context: This long passage completes the sustained warning against adultery begun in chapter 5. It opens with tender, almost domestic language, bind my teaching to your heart like a lamp for your path, then turns severe, comparing adultery to embracing fire, and finally becomes a short story: a father, watching from his window, narrates in painful detail how a naive young man is lured, seduced, and led like an animal to slaughter by a bold, deceptive woman.
basic.hermeneutics: The shift from direct command, 6:20 to 7:5, to narrated scene, 7:6 to 23, is a deliberate literary strategy. By showing rather than merely telling, the passage lets the reader watch temptation unfold in real time, twilight, a chance encounter, flattering words, an appeal to false piety, until the reader feels the same mounting momentum the young man does, and then meets the same abrupt, fatal outcome. The warning is not against womanhood or sexuality but against a specific pattern, seductive deception meeting an unguarded heart.
basic.originalLanguages: The nokriyah, adulteress, is literally 'the foreign one,' a woman made a stranger to her own marriage covenant through unfaithfulness. The husband's qinah, jealousy, in 6:34 names a righteous, unappeasable anger no bribe can satisfy, showing adultery is treated as a real wound to real people, not a private matter without cost. And the young man is a peti, one lacking judgment, 7:7, not stupid but unguarded, exactly the condition wisdom's teaching exists to correct.
basic.typology: The young man led 'like an ox going to the slaughter,' 7:22, uses the same image later applied to the Suffering Servant, who was 'led like a lamb to the slaughter,' Isaiah 53:7, a jarring contrast between a fool destroyed by his own folly and the innocent Christ who willingly bore a death He did not deserve for the very folly this passage describes.
basic.christ: This passage exposes how easily an unguarded heart is led toward destruction, following its own desires 'like an ox going to the slaughter.' The gospel takes up that very image and reverses it: Christ, wholly innocent, was 'led like a lamb to the slaughter,' Isaiah 53:7, not because He was deceived or unguarded but because He willingly bore the judgment that the folly and unfaithfulness this passage describes truly deserve. Where the young man's path led to Sheol, Christ entered death itself and rose, opening a road back to life for all who had wandered.
basic.god: God cares intensely about marital faithfulness, treating adultery's cost as real and severe, wounds, dishonor, and an unappeasable jealousy, not a private matter with no wider consequence. Yet He is also the source of the very instruction, likened to a lamp and a light, 6:23, that guards against this destruction, showing His concern to protect, not merely to condemn.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 53:7 pictures the Suffering Servant led like a lamb to the slaughter, a deliberate contrast to the fool of 7:22. Matthew 5:28 extends the seventh commandment to the heart's desire, echoing 6:25. Hebrews 13:4 upholds the honor of marriage and the certainty of God's judgment on the unfaithful. James 1:14 to 15 traces the same trajectory from desire to sin to death.
basic.oneStory: This passage's vivid warning against a heart unguarded by wisdom, ending in a road to death, belongs to Scripture's larger story of humanity's wandering desires and the ruin they bring. That story finds its rescue in Christ, who was led to the slaughter not for His own folly but for ours, so that the road to Sheol this passage describes might be closed and a road back to life opened for all who turn to Him.

context.literary: The passage moves from direct paternal command, 6:20 to 7:5, into an extended narrated scene, 7:6 to 23, one of the most developed pieces of storytelling in the book, before returning to direct address in the closing warning, 7:24 to 27, a literary arc that intensifies the warning by letting the reader witness temptation as it happens rather than merely hearing it described.
context.historical: The woman's claim to have just offered peace offerings and paid her vows, 7:14, reflects an ancient practice where portions of certain sacrifices were eaten in a festive meal soon after offering them, she uses genuine religious language and festive provision as a cover for seduction, a chilling picture of piety used to mask sin.
context.canonical: This passage's climax, a young man led like an ox to the slaughter, 7:22, is deliberately echoed and reversed in Isaiah's portrait of the Servant led like a lamb to the slaughter, 53:7, one of the clearest verbal links between wisdom literature's picture of self destructive folly and the New Testament's picture of Christ's innocent, substitutionary death.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To press the danger of adultery home so vividly, through direct command and then narrated scene, that the reader feels the mounting momentum of temptation and its abrupt, fatal cost, and so treasures wisdom's instruction as the guard that keeps the heart from ever taking that first step down her road.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The commands to keep, treasure, and guard wisdom's teaching are directly prescriptive. The narrated scene of the young man's seduction, 7:6 to 23, is a vivid, typical case study meant to illustrate the general pattern and end of unguarded desire, not a claim that every act of adultery follows this exact sequence of events; its power lies in showing the ordinary, gradual shape temptation usually takes.

debates.question: Is the woman of chapter 7 a literal figure, a symbol, or both?

views.label: A real, literal danger
views.case: The passage's concrete details, a real house, real perfumes, a real absent husband, read as realistic warning against an actual social danger young men faced in ancient Israel.

views.label: Also a symbol of folly generally
views.case: Set against Wisdom personified elsewhere in these chapters, chapters 1, 8, and 9, the adulteress functions as folly's counter figure, representing any seductive path that promises life and delivers death, not sexual sin exclusively.

debates.bearing: Both readings work together, the passage addresses a real and serious social danger while also functioning, within the book's larger structure, as one vivid instance of the wider pattern in which folly disguises itself as desirable and wisdom's voice must be treasured to see through the disguise.

study.originalLanguages: The nokriyah, adulteress, is literally the woman made foreign or estranged through broken covenant, 6:24, 7:5. The husband's qinah, jealousy, 6:34, is a righteous, unappeasable anger that no bribe can satisfy, underscoring how seriously ancient Israel treated the violation of marriage. And the watched young man is called peti, one lacking judgment, 7:7, a condition of dangerous naivety rather than incurable stupidity, precisely the condition this whole passage's teaching is meant to remedy before it is too late.

typology.type: A young man led like an ox to the slaughter, Proverbs 7:22
typology.fulfillment: Reversed and fulfilled in the innocent Suffering Servant, led like a lamb to the slaughter though guilty of nothing, Isaiah 53:7.

study.christ: The passage's most haunting image, a young man following his own unguarded desire 'like an ox going to the slaughter,' 7:22, is deliberately answered by Isaiah's picture of the Servant who was 'led like a lamb to the slaughter' and 'like a sheep before its shearers is silent,' Isaiah 53:7. The difference could not be sharper: the young man is led by his own folly to a death he did not need to die, while Christ, entirely without folly or sin, was led to a death He did not deserve, bearing the very judgment this passage says such folly earns. Because He walked that road willingly, everyone who has ever wandered toward the adulteress's house, in this sin or any other, can find the road to Sheol closed and a road to life opened in Him.
study.god: God treats the marriage bond and its violation with the utmost seriousness, His own instruction is likened to a lamp and light meant to protect people from real, devastating harm, and He recognizes the very real anger and grief unfaithfulness causes between husband and wife. He is also patient enough to narrate, in loving detail, exactly how temptation unfolds, so that His people might recognize and resist it before the point of no return.

crossReferences.note: The Suffering Servant led like a lamb to the slaughter, a deliberate reversal of the fool led like an ox in 7:22.
Jesus extends the command against adultery to the heart's desire, echoing 6:25's warning against lusting in the heart.
Marriage is to be held in honor, and God will judge the adulterer, affirming the seriousness of this passage's warning.
Desire, when it has conceived, gives birth to sin, and sin, when full grown, brings forth death, tracing the same trajectory as this passage's narrative.

study.oneStory: This passage's story of an unguarded young man led step by step to his own destruction pictures the whole trajectory of sin, sweet at first, deadly in the end. That story is answered by another figure led to slaughter, not a fool destroyed by his own folly but the innocent Christ, who bore the judgment folly deserves so that the road to death this passage describes could be closed forever for all who take refuge in Him.

quiz.q: What are the father's teachings compared to in 6:22-23?

options.text: A lamp and a light that guide, guard, and speak throughout the day and night.
options.note: This is the exact image given, a lamp for walking, watching over sleep, and speaking upon waking.

options.text: A burden that restricts freedom.
options.note: The passage presents this teaching as protective guidance, not a restrictive burden.

options.text: A one time warning needed only once in youth.
options.note: The imagery of guiding by day and watching by night suggests continual, ongoing guidance, not a single early warning.

options.text: Advice useful only for financial decisions.
options.note: The immediate context applies this specifically to guarding against the adulteress, not finances.

quiz.q: How does 6:27-29 argue that adultery cannot be entered without cost?

options.text: By comparing it to embracing fire or walking on hot coals, both certain to burn.
options.note: Verses 27-28 use exactly this vivid double comparison.

options.text: By showing that some adulterers escape all consequences entirely.
options.note: The passage instead insists no one who touches another man's wife will go unpunished, verse 29.

options.text: By comparing it to a minor, easily healed injury.
options.note: The imagery of fire and hot coals suggests serious, not minor, harm.

options.text: By focusing only on financial loss.
options.note: Financial and social cost are named elsewhere, verses 31, 33, but this specific argument uses the fire and coals image for inevitable, serious harm generally, not finances alone.

quiz.q: In 7:14, what does the woman claim to have just done, and why is this significant?

options.text: She claims to have just offered peace offerings and paid her vows, using religious language and provision as a cover for seduction.
options.note: This detail shows how piety itself can be twisted into a mask for sin, a chilling feature of the scene.

options.text: She claims to be a foreigner with no local ties.
options.note: Her claim concerns sacrifices and vows, not her nationality.

options.text: She claims her husband has died.
options.note: She actually claims her husband is away on a journey, not deceased, verse 19.

options.text: She claims to be seeking legal advice.
options.note: Her stated purpose is explicitly seduction, dressed in religious language, not a legal matter.

quiz.q: What image describes the young man's fate in 7:22-23?

options.text: Like an ox going to the slaughter, not knowing it will cost him his life.
options.note: This is the passage's exact climactic image.

options.text: Like a shepherd leading his flock safely home.
options.note: This reverses the passage's actual image of being led toward destruction, not safety.

options.text: Like a soldier bravely facing a known danger.
options.note: The passage stresses his lack of awareness, not knowing, not deliberate bravery in the face of a known risk.

options.text: Like a merchant securing a profitable trade.
options.note: The passage's image is of unknowing destruction, not profit or gain of any kind.

quiz.q: How does the image of being 'led like an ox to the slaughter' relate to Christ?

options.text: It is reversed in Isaiah's picture of the innocent Servant led like a lamb to the slaughter, bearing judgment He did not deserve.
options.note: Isaiah 53:7 uses strikingly similar language for the Suffering Servant, in deliberate contrast to this fool's self caused ruin.

options.text: It shows that Christ was also guilty of folly like the young man in this passage.
options.note: Isaiah 53 explicitly presents the Servant as innocent, unlike the foolish young man here.

options.text: It has no connection to any New Testament image.
options.note: The slaughter imagery is picked up directly and significantly in Isaiah's Servant Songs, which the New Testament applies to Christ.

options.text: It only describes Old Testament sacrificial animals, with no further meaning.
options.note: This underplays the deliberate literary echo between a fool led to his own ruin and the innocent Servant led to bear others' ruin.

questions: The father describes his teaching as a lamp guiding both day and night. What ongoing, daily practice keeps God's word active as a guiding light in your life, rather than a lesson learned once and forgotten?

The woman in chapter 7 used religious language, having just paid her vows, as cover for seduction. Where might you be tempted to use spiritual language or activity to justify something you know is wrong?

The young man walked toward danger at twilight, in that vulnerable space between clear day and full dark. What are the twilight moments or situations in your own life where you are most vulnerable to temptation?
He followed her 'on impulse,' not realizing the cost until it was too late. What decision are you facing right now where you need to pause and count the cost before acting on impulse?

Christ was led like a lamb to the slaughter for sins He did not commit, so that those who wander toward ruin could be rescued. Where do you need to receive His rescue today, rather than trying to escape a snare on your own strength?

translationNotes.note: The levy of the prostitute renders a Hebrew phrase literally referring to the price paid for a loaf of bread, picturing prostitution's cost as reducing a man to poverty.
Apple of your eye renders a Hebrew idiom literally meaning the pupil, an image for something guarded with the utmost care and attentiveness.
The Hebrew of this verse is difficult, and the rendering here follows the Septuagint, Vulgate, and Syriac; the Hebrew text as it stands reads more literally 'like fetters to discipline a fool.'

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Proverbs 6:20 through 7:27. Poetic line breaks in the source were rendered as flowing prose; one em dash present at 7:23 was rewritten using a comma to comply with the no dash rule; the wording is otherwise unchanged and complete.
